#core/appliedneuroscience

![bio-electrical-activities](_attachments/bio-electrical-activities.png)

**Biological electrical activity spans an extraordinary range of voltages** — from graded synaptic events of a few millivolts to the hundreds of volts discharged by specialised electric organs. Four canonical types cover this range.

## Large voltages generated by animals

- Specialised electric organs — such as those of **electric eels and electric rays** — can discharge **hundreds of volts**; classic texts cite up to ~700 V, while *Electrophorus voltai* holds the confirmed record at **860 V** (de Santana et al., 2019).
- Produced by stacks of electrocytes (modified muscle cells) whose membrane potentials sum in series, like cells wired in a battery.

## Negative resting membrane potential

- Most neurons maintain a **[resting membrane potential](resting_membrane_potential.md) of around -70 mV**, the interior of the cell being negative relative to the exterior.
- Established by unequal [concentration gradients](concentration_gradients.md) of ions across the membrane and maintained by the Na+/K+-ATPase pump together with leak [ion channels](ion_channels.md).

## Postsynaptic potentials

- **Small, graded, variable changes in membrane potential (≈1–40 mV; typically only a few mV at the soma of central neurons)** generated at synapses.
- Either excitatory or inhibitory — see [IPSPs and EPSPs](ipsps_and_epsps.md) — shifting the membrane away from rest via [hyperpolarisation or depolarisation](hyperpolarisation_and_depolarisation.md).
- Unlike action potentials, they are **proportional to stimulus strength**, sum together, and decay with distance.

## Action potentials

- **Large (~100 mV), fast, all-or-none spikes**, fired once graded input depolarises the membrane past threshold.
- The fundamental unit of long-range neural signalling; because their amplitude is stereotyped, information is encoded in **spike timing and rate**, not size.

## Summary

| Type | Typical magnitude | Character |
| ---- | ----------------- | --------- |
| Electric-organ discharge | up to ~860 V | Specialised whole-organ event |
| Resting membrane potential | ≈ -70 mV | Standing baseline voltage |
| Postsynaptic potentials | ≈ 1–40 mV | Graded, variable, local |
| Action potentials | ~100 mV | Fast, all-or-none, propagating |

## From single cells to networks

Individual potentials become experimentally accessible when summed across populations:

- Synchronised postsynaptic potentials in cortical pyramidal neurons generate the **oscillations** detected by EEG; population activity more generally is recorded as a [field potential](field_potential.md).
- The ladder of extracellular configurations — field potentials, whole nerve, multi-unit, single units, MEAs — is compared in [Summary of extracellular recordings](summary_of_extracellular_recordings.md), and the underlying measurement trade-offs in [Electrophysiology measurements](electrophysiology_measurements.md).
